Poupatempo Paraná: Comprovante de Situação Cadastral no CPF

CPF Status Consultation as a Self-Service Experience

Role
Lead Product Designer
Year
2025
Company
Citizen Experience (VFS Company)

Context and Challenges

Citizens can already check their CPF status through the federal government portal. The CPF (Cadastro de Pessoas Físicas) is Brazil's national individual taxpayer identification number and is required to access most public services, benefits, and formal processes in the country.

However, this verification required leaving the Poupatempo ecosystem and navigating a separate platform with different language, flows, and authentication rules. In practice, this break in experience often caused confusion, drop-offs, and a return to assisted service.

The goal of this project was to bring this essential verification into a single, unified platform, allowing citizens to complete the entire CPF consultation journey within Poupatempo's app or kiosks, with a consistent, simple, and accessible experience.

My Role

As a Product Designer, I led the design of this service end to end. I was responsible for research, service and flow design, prototyping, internal testing, and iteration in collaboration with product and engineering teams.

Discovery and Definition

The work started with understanding recurring citizen demands related to CPF verification and observing how people interacted with existing self-service kiosks. The main insight was clear: users were not interested in understanding systems or rules, they simply wanted a clear answer, fast, and without ambiguity.

Based on this, I designed a short and linear flow focused on clarity and accessibility. Language was kept simple and direct, avoiding technical terms. Special attention was given to system feedback, error states, and confirmation messages, ensuring that users with different levels of digital literacy could complete the service confidently.

The same experience logic was applied to both the app and the kiosk, adapting only what was necessary for each context, such as touch targets, readability, and usage time.

Prototyping & Testing

I developed interactive prototypes for both channels and conducted internal usability tests with Poupatempo staff. These tests helped refine content hierarchy, messaging, and visual feedback, reducing friction and uncertainty throughout the flow.

Outcome and Key Learning

The final solution enabled citizens to check CPF status autonomously and generate a proof document without assisted service. The project reduced operational load, improved service efficiency, and delivered a consistent experience across digital and physical touchpoints.

Designing public services reinforced that simplicity is not a lack of depth — it is a deliberate design decision that directly impacts accessibility, trust, and scale.

Poupatempo Paraná: Defensoria Pública

Digitizing Public Defender Services in the Poupatempo Paraná platforms

Role
Product Designer
Year
2025
Company
Citizen Experience (VFS Company)

Context and Challenges

The Public Defender's Office of the State of Paraná (DPE-PR) provides free and comprehensive legal assistance to individuals and families with a monthly household income of up to three minimum wages. Its mission is to ensure access to justice for people in vulnerable situations, operating across areas such as family law, civil law, criminal defense, housing, health, and the protection of women, children, and adolescents.

This case study describes a project carried out as part of the Poupatempo Paraná digital platform. Poupatempo Paraná is a state initiative focused on simplifying access to public services by offering digital entry points that reduce bureaucracy and dependence on in-person attendance.

Within this context, the project focused on dematerializing key services of the Public Defender's Office, transforming processes that were previously dependent on face-to-face interactions, physical documents, and complex legal language into structured, digital, and user-centered experiences.

The main challenge was to translate legally complex and sensitive procedures into flows that were clear, safe, and accessible, without compromising legal validity, institutional requirements, or user trust.

The services redesigned and made available digitally were:

  • Child support, custody, and visitation support
  • Paternity investigation support
  • Divorce support
  • Dissolution of stable unions support
  • Domestic violence against women support

By redesigning these services for a digital environment, the project aimed to reduce access barriers, support informed decision-making, and provide a more humane and inclusive path to justice for citizens in vulnerable situations.

My Role

I worked as a Product Designer as part of a design team of four designers. I contributed across multiple stages of the design process, collaborating closely with Developers, Product Managers, Requirements Analysts, and external stakeholders.

My responsibilities included conducting user research, mapping user flows, producing design documentation, and creating low- and high-fidelity prototypes to support alignment, decision-making, and implementation.

Discovery and Definition

The discovery phase focused on building a deep and reliable understanding of both the institutional context and the legal framework surrounding the Public Defender's Office.

We conducted extensive desk research covering the structure, responsibilities, and service scope of the Public Defender's Office, as well as a thorough review of state and federal legislation related to each service.

To validate and refine this understanding, we held multiple working sessions with public defenders and lawyers from the institution. These meetings were used to confirm legal interpretations, clarify edge cases, and validate the accuracy of service flows, forms, and terminology.

In parallel, we ran a survey with volunteers to evaluate the clarity and usability of the proposed forms.

Special care was taken with highly sensitive services, such as domestic violence against women. In these cases, tests and surveys were conducted exclusively with women, ensuring a safer environment to evaluate tone, clarity, emotional impact, and potential triggers within the forms.

Prototype Development

I worked on the creation of the project's mobile and self-service kiosk prototypes, leveraging the app's design system and component library. The prototypes represented complete user flows and supported validation, alignment with stakeholders, and development handoff.

User Feedback & Refinement

Facilitated user testing with a diverse group of app users, gathering insightful feedback on the redesign. This phase was crucial in identifying unforeseen usability issues and validating the effectiveness of the new design elements.

© 2026 Mariana Balster

Amazon Studios Submission Portal

Improving scalability and internal workflows for original content submissions in Latin America

Role
Research Intern
Years
2021 – 2023
Company
Prime Video & Amazon MGM Studios

Context

In 2021, at Prime Video and Amazon MGM Studios, the volume of original content proposals submitted by creators across Latin America was rapidly increasing.

At the time, submissions were received and managed manually via email, creating operational strain for internal teams responsible for reviewing, tracking, and evaluating projects.

This process lacked structure, scalability, and visibility, making it difficult to handle growing demand while maintaining consistent evaluation criteria and communication.

Problem

While supporting the intake and organization of project submissions, I observed firsthand that the existing email-based workflow would not scale to accommodate the increasing number of proposals.

Key issues included:

  • High volume of unstructured submissions
  • Manual tracking and follow-ups
  • Limited visibility into submission status
  • Inefficient handoffs between internal teams

Solution

The team designed and launched a Content Submission Portal, a dedicated digital platform that centralized incoming proposals and standardized the evaluation workflow.

As a Research Intern, I supported the project by:

  • Contributing to user flow discussions
  • Assisting in prototype development
  • Supporting user testing activities
  • Collaborating with designers, product managers, and engineers across Latin America and the United States

Outcome & Impact

  • Reduced internal evaluation time by up to 60%
  • Received 100+ submissions in the first week after launch
  • Became the standard intake channel for original content proposals in Brazil

What I learned

  • Identify product opportunities through operational observation
  • Translate real-world constraints into research insights
  • Collaborate within large, cross-functional, international teams
  • Contribute meaningfully to product outcomes even in an early-career role
→ Access portal

© 2026 Mariana Balster

I'm drawn to complexity.

I'm a Senior UX Designer with a background that spans digital platforms, civic technology, and embedded systems. What drives me isn't just making things look good. It's making hard problems feel simple for the people who face them every day.

I thrive in environments where the constraints are real, the stakes matter, and the solution space isn't obvious. Whether that means navigating legal frameworks to digitize public defender services, designing for self-service kiosks used by vulnerable populations, or mapping user flows across fragmented systems. I find energy in work that requires both rigor and empathy.

I believe great design lives at the intersection of strategic thinking and deep user understanding. I'm comfortable leading research, facilitating workshops, writing specs, and working closely with engineers from discovery through delivery.
